You've just finished training a decision tree for spam classification, and it is getting abnormally bad performance on both your training and test sets. You know that your implementation has no bugs, so what could be causing the problem?

choices.B: Your decision trees are too deep.
choices.C: You need to increase the learning rate.
choices.D: Your data is not properly normalized.
choices.G: You need to decrease the learning rate.
choices.H: Your decision trees are too shallow.

questions.question: What would you do in PCA to get the same projection as SVD?

choices.E: Subtract the median from each data point
choices.G: Apply a Fourier transform to the data
choices.H: Apply a logarithmic transformation to the data

questions.question: _ refers to a model that can neither model the training data nor generalize to new data.

Statement 1| VGGNets have convolutional kernels of smaller width and height than AlexNet's first-layer kernels. Statement 2| Data-dependent weight initialization procedures were introduced before Batch Normalization.

choices.A: Both Statement 1 and Statement 2 are Not Given
choices.B: Statement 1 is Not Given and Statement 2 is True
choices.D: Statement 1 is False and Statement 2 is Not Given
choices.G: Statement 1 is True and Statement 2 is Not Given
choices.I: Statement 1 is Not Given and Statement 2 is False
choices.J: Statement 1 is Not Given and Statement 2 is Not Given

questions.question: Consider the Bayesian network given below. How many independent parameters would we need if we made no assumptions about independence or conditional independence H -> U <- P <- W?
Which among the following prevents overfitting when we perform bagging?

choices.A: The use of all data without sampling
choices.B: The practice of validation performed on a random subset of classifiers trained
choices.C: The use of underfitting to counterbalance overfitting
choices.E: The use of classification algorithms which are prone to overfitting
choices.F: The use of classification algorithms which are not prone to overfitting
choices.G: The use of sampling with replacement as the sampling technique
choices.H: The practice of validation performed on every classifier trained
choices.J: The use of sampling without replacement as the sampling technique

questions.question: For Kernel Regression, which one of these structural assumptions is the one that most affects the trade-off between underfitting and overfitting:

choices.A: The type of regression model used
choices.B: Whether kernel function is Gaussian versus triangular versus box-shaped
choices.C: Whether we use Euclidian versus L1 versus L∞ metrics
choices.D: The distance function used in the kernel
choices.F: The number of data points used in the regression
choices.G: The minimum height of the kernel function
choices.I: The maximum height of the kernel function
choices.J: The shape of the kernel function's tails

questions.question: For a Gaussian Bayes classifier, which one of these structural assumptions is the one that most affects the trade-off between underfitting and overfitting:

choices.A: Whether we learn the class centers by Maximum Likelihood or Gradient Descent
choices.B: Whether we assume full class covariance matrices or diagonal class covariance matrices
choices.C: Whether we have equal class priors or priors estimated from the data.
choices.D: Whether we allow classes to have different mean vectors or we force them to share the same mean vector

questions.question: Statement 1| For any two variables x and y having joint distribution p(x, y), we always have H[x, y] ≥ H[x] + H[y] where H is entropy function. Statement 2| For some directed graphs, moralization decreases the number of edges present in the graph.

choices.J: Not enough information, Not enough information

questions.question: We are training fully connected network with two hidden layers to predict housing prices. Inputs are $100$-dimensional, and have several features such as the number of square feet, the median family income, etc. The first hidden layer has $1000$ activations. The second hidden layer has $10$ activations. The output is a scalar representing the house price. Assuming a vanilla network with affine transformations and with no batch normalization and no learnable parameters in the activation function, how many parameters does this network have?
Which of the following sentence is FALSE regarding regression?

choices.A: It may be used for interpretation.
